heading: Course Description
text:
objective course expose students modern bioinformatics
studies specific focus analysis next generation sequencing

heading: Good examples
text:
first sentence explains (determine whether changes
DNA methylation associated changes gene expression), 
(measured transcript levels using mRNA-Seq). Following sentences explain
results:
code:
To determine whether changes in DNA methylation were associated with
changes in gene expression, we measured transcript levels using mRNA-Seq.
Overall, the genes we find to be differentially regulated have a high
degree of overlap (P < 1E-109) with a previous study (27) that used
microarrays (Fig. S1F). Genes that overlap in both studies and decrease in
expression in the STHdhQ111 cells are associated with developmental
processes, neuron migration, regulation of signaling, and regulation of
neural precursor cell proliferation (Dataset S2). Genes that increase in
expression in the STHdhQ111 cells in both our study and the previously
published microarray data are associated with categories including
extracellular matrix organization, signal transduction, and cell
differentiation (Dataset S2). [Ng, Christopher W., et al. "Extensive
changes in DNA methylation are associated with expression of mutant
huntingtin." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences110.6 (2013):
2354-2359.]

heading: Example 1
text:
Original:Purpose unclear. Many references given without prior mentioning. Methods
clear. use term “cross validation” explained
clearly.Rewritten:
code:
Then a set of Welch T test (with unequal variance) was conducted between
same genes in different clusters, and the p values for the tests were
adjusted with the FDR value. 1000 genes were filtered out to be the most
significant markers for the variances between two clusters. Within these
1000 genes, the analyst did a cross validation with the supplementary
material provided by the original paper. In the supplementary material
there is a table of the logFC data of cancer vs control, the analyst
checked the data and located genes with large differences in the number of
fold changes vs control (for example, genes have large positive value in
logFC in C3 vs Control and have a very negative value in logFC in C4 vs
Control at the same time and vice versa), and check if these genes are in
the 1000 genes located from the adjusted p value filter.To determine if the genes expression in the detected different patient
clusters have similar distributions, we applied Welch T test on the sample
genes present in different clusters. The top 1000 genes with adjusted
p-value<xxx were selected as the markers for the clusters. We compared
these genes with the fold change values given by authors. [The criteria for
the validation is unclear and should be rewritten]

heading: Example 2
text:
Original:CSV R version whatever much detail add flow
(part accuracy). text “least 20% samples” ambiguous. 
expression greater log2(15), mean least 15 reads? 
normalization method get expression values explained , remove
log2 just place number. Rewrite sentences make method clear.
Keep numbers within reasonable precision (part accuracy), example
20% samples, log2(15), 0.01, 0.186. choose 0.186?Rewritten:
code:
Data was read as CSV format, as comma separated files. Based on Marisa et
al. we selected genes using three well defined metrics and these filters
were applied to the RMA normalized and ComBat adjusted expression matrix
data for this analysis. These filters included; (1) Genes expressed in at
least 20% of the samples with expression values greater than log2(15); (2)
Have a variance significantly different from the median variance of all
probe sets using a threshold of p<0.01 then applying Chi-square statistic;
and (3) genes with a coefficient of variation greater than 0.186.
Additionally, a quantile chi-square test was applied to the dataset with
degrees of freedom (N-1) and alpha level equal to 0.01. All filtering
analysis were done on R version 3.5.1 with built in packages in Rstudio.Variable genes were selected based on the three variability metrics
suggested in Marisa et. al. First, genes were required to have a minimum of
15 reads in at least xxx samples (20% of all samples). Then, the genes were
filtered based on their variance to ensure they have significantly higher
variance than an average gene. The variance was computed by a chi-square
test. Genes with a coefficient of variation greater than 0.186 at p-value <
0.01 were selected.  These genes were used in the downstream analysis.

heading: Example 3
text: Original:Rewritten:
code:
It is interesting to see if any certain feature of a TR, i.e. pattern
length, copy number or annotation would be predictive of VNTRs. In order to
study that, we fitted a generalized regression model (glm) to predict
whether a TR is VNTR, or if any VNTR will become common or hyper.We constructed generalized linear models to determine whether specific TR
features, e.g., pattern length, copy number, array length, or genomic
annotation, are predictive of VNTR status.  Four models were designed, one
to predict whether a TR is a VNTR and three more to predict VNTR subtypes
(private, common, hyper).

heading: Good example
text:
methods section Mandelboum et al
2019,
subdivided clarity:study design (# samples, RNA-Seq datasets) clearly defined, 
rationale sample selection.Specific sources data mentioned without providing much detail (GEO
SRA DB). Specific reference genomes mentioned (hg19, mm10), 
published methods mentioned correctly cited (TopHat2, FeatureCounts).
Appropriate detail included important minimum cpm parameter (1.0 
replicates least one biological condition).Concise language appropriate citations, inclusion relevant analysis
choice details (e.g. “genes multiple transcripts…”) without much
implementation detail.
code:
We analyzed 35 publicly available human or mouse RNA-seq datasets from GEO
[30] (S1 Table). We sought datasets that were (1) published in recent years
(mostly in 2017-18), (2) contained 2-4 replicate samples of each biological
condition, (3) probed treatments with well-documented biological responses
(e.g., TNFa) to ease functional interpretation and recognition of true
calls by GSEA, and (4) collectively covered diverse biological processes.We downloaded either raw count data files when provided by GEO or,
otherwise, raw sequence fastq files (from SRA DB). In the latter, reads
were aligned to the reference genome (hg19 for Hs and mm10 for Mm) using
TopHat2 [31], and gene count data were generated using FeatureCounts [32].
We calculated cpm levels, and in each dataset analysis, we included only
the expressed genes (defined as those whose expression was at least 1.0 cpm
in all replicate samples of at least one of the biological condition probed
in the dataset).Following this filtering step, gene counts were normalized using six
different normalization methods: RPKM [7], RPKM followed by quantile
normalization [25], TMM [8], RLE [9], RLE followed by RPKM, and UQ
normalization followed by RPKM [11], all implemented in edgeR [33]. cqn and
EDASeq (both available as Bioconductor packages) were applied to expression
count data. Gene-expression FC was either calculated by dividing normalized
expression levels (after adding 1.0 to both numerator and denominator and
averaging over replicate samples in the treatment versus control
comparisons) or estimated by edgeR regression model fit. Gene annotations
were downloaded from GENCODE (v25 for Hs and vM10 for Mm) [34]. For genes
with multiple transcripts, we took the length of the principal transcript
(as defined by GENCODE's annotation of principal and alternative splice
isoforms [APPRIS] annotations [35]) or the length of the longest transcript
if principal transcript is not defined for the gene. All statistical
analyses were performed in R. Statistical significance of Spearman's
correlation was calculated using the cor.test function.

heading: Average examples
text:
Example 1Original:Writing concise, little description data, much description methods.Rewritten:Example 2Original:Unnecessary general description methods section, much implementation
detail, Methods text generally include bullet points.Rewritten:
code:
On the GEO (Gene Expression Omnibus) website, the paper has the repository
with accession Number GSE64403. The data was acquired by using the SRA
toolkit. The sample data GSM1570702 was downloaded using the fastq-dump
``--split-files``. The ``--split-files`` is the argument that split the SRA file
into two fastq files.Paired end RNA-Seq data for the P0 timepoint sample (Dataset Accession
GSM1570702) was downloaded using SRA toolkit from GEO (Series Accession
GSE64403).The mapping process of the two give FASTQ files containing the RNA-seq
paired end reads needed index files for the barcodes used in the RNA-Seq,
in addition to annotation file of the reference sequence. Due to the
computationally demanding nature of this process, a batch job script was
coded to utilize number of tools available on the Shared Computer Cluster
(SCC) (Table 5):

- Bowtie2: is a package for alignment based on Barrows-Wheeler algorithm,
  which allows a margin of mismatch and randomization that reduces the
  memory footprint. And therefor it is fast and described as greedy
  algorithm. Bowtie is not Reads splice aware [6].
- Tophat v2.1.1: is a splice junction mapper for RNA-Seq reads based on the
  aligner bowtie. Its job is to identify splice junctions between exons
  (Figure 5).
- Boost v1.58.0 is C++ library for string and text processing.
- Samtools V0.1.19: are tools to view, sort, merge and to get statistics on
  alignment files, that are kept in SAM format.Paired end FASTQ files were aligned against the mm9 reference genome with
Bowtie2[6], followed by spliced alignment with Tophat[citation needed].
Alignment statistics were calculated using samtools.

code:
1. Make a new project directory for this project
2. Create a new conda environment with the following packages:
3. Snakemake, pandas, jupyter-lab, fastqc, trimmomatic, bowtie2, samtools, HOMER, bedtools, deeptools
    - N.B. Some tools may be stored under a different name in bioconda
4. Export your environment description to a .txt file
5. Initialize your project directory as a git repository and commit the following files:
    - Your conda environment description .txt file
    - A short, descriptive README
    - The provided .gitignore (you may also copy the .gitignore from the first project)1. Navigate to the EMBL ENA website (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browser/home) and search in the top right for our GEO accession, GSE75070. Open up the “Project” page for the results returned.
2. Download the report in the “Read Files” section as a .TSV and upload this file to your working directory using a sFTP or the OnDemand interface.
    - This file is a standard .TSV with fields containing sample metadata information, and importantly for us, FTP links to the full FASTQ files. 
3. Write a snakemake rule that parses this .TSV file and extracts out the FTP paths for only the ChIPseq samples (SRR2919474, SRR2919475, SRR2919476, SRR2919477). Write only the FTP paths to a new file with each separated by a newline character. 
4. Write a second snakemake rule that takes this file of FTP paths and downloads each one
    - `man wget` or `man curl` will outline strategies for downloading multiple files listed in a single .txt1. Rename the 4 downloaded files to work with your snakemake workflow
    - You may use the naming strategy suggested or your own
    - You do not need to do this in snakemake1. Download the gencode human primary assembly fasta and matching GTF annotations
    - This ChIPseq experiment was performed in MCF-7 cells, which are an immortalized human cell line. Always quickly check to make sure that you are using the appropriate species’ reference genome when processing publicly available sequencing data. 

2. Generate a snakemake rule (or two) that download the latest gencode human primary assembly reference genome and its matching GTF file (GRCh38.p14)

3. Generate a genome index with bowtie21. Please read the bowtie2 manual and specifically the section covering the command bowtie2-build 
2. Generate a bowtie2 index using the downloaded primary assembly fasta
    - Leave all parameters at default
    - Include a threads directive in your snakemake rule and request 8. 1. Create a snakemake rule that runs fastQC on our samples1. Create a snakemake rule that runs trimmomatic on the FASTQ files
    - For the provided adapter FASTA, please use the following file:
    - Leave all other parameters at their suggested defaults for single end reads

Read manual pages samtools sort samtools index commands use ., perform quick quality check alignment using samtools collect QC information generated far using MultiQC.Now performed basic quality control, going convert aligned BAM files tractable format analysis visualization. using deeptools package convert information BAM files bigWig format, compressed binary indexed file typically used store quantitative data associated genomic regions. essence, can divide every chromosome bins equal known size count number alignments falling region. enables simple intuitive plot can visualize entire length arbitrary region genome number alignments found position. enables us look “signal” coming various regions genome.often used ChIPseq experiments visualize factor interest binding. Regions higher alignment counts, underlying molecular biology experiment worked, represent regions factor likely binding. Understanding genome factor binding enables determination genes factor regulating, mechanisms functions.can also use information make general quantitative comparisons samples since unified format (.e. sample represents entire genome split sized bins associated count). use property perform Pearson correlation signal generated samples. ideal world, experiment worked exactly intended, biological replicates correctly capturing Runx1 binding sites, signal represented bigWig format exactly least, highly correlated. Given knowledge input control represents, also assume inputs substantially different IP samples.end second week, accomplished following:
- Aligned reads using bowtie2 previously created bowtie2 genome index latest Gencode human reference genome
- command, automatically convert output bowtie2 (SAM) BAM file using samtools
- Create snakemake rule sorts BAM files
- Create snakemake rule generates BAM index file sorted BAM files

- Create snakemake rule runs MultiQC aggregate outputs QC utilities

code:
1. Align your reads to the human reference genome using the index you created last week 
    - You may leave all other bowtie2 parameters at default or justify if you change any
    - In the same command, automatically convert the bowtie2 output to a BAM file using samtools1. Generate a snakemake rule that sorts each of your BAM files
2. Generate another snakemake rule that creates a BAM index for your sorted files1. Create a snakemake rule that runs samtools flagstat on all of your aligned files
2. Create a snakemake rule that runs MultiQC on your project directory1. Using the bamcoverage utility in deeptools, generate a bigWig file for each sorted BAM using default parameters
2. Once you have produced all of the bigwig files, use the multiBigWigSummary utility and the plotCorrelation utility to produce a clustered heatmap of the Pearson correlation values between all the samples. Refer to their respective manual pages here for help: https://deeptools.readthedocs.io/en/develop/content/list_of_tools.html. 
    - Compare your figure to the one found in the paper Supp Fig 2b. Make sure to think about some of the following questions: 
    - How similar are your correlation values? 
    - What could cause these differences to arise? 
    - Do they affect the overall conclusion drawn from this particular figure? 
    - What is the overall conclusion made from this figure by the authors?

Peak calling

Please read documentation findPeaks command (http://homer.ucsd.edu/homer/ngs/peaks.html) perform peak calling replicate experiments.output findPeaks homer-specific format, ease use, convert .txt homer peak file standard BED formatted file.paired experiment (IP INP) replicate generated set peaks represent likely Runx1 binding locations genome. general, two main ways “reproducible” peaks chosen ChIP-seq experiments: 1. page, change options prompted download BED file containing requested information. Put BED file working directory SCC. simple use case, UCSC table browser UCSC genome browser incredibly powerful tools repositories genome-wide sequencing data.end week 3, accomplished following:
- Generated snakemake rule runs makeTagDir utility sorted BAM files
- Create snakemake rule runs HOMER findPeaks samples using tag directories generated previously
- generate peak file set replicates (IP rep1: INP rep1 IP rep2: INP rep2)
- Created snakemake rule converts findPeaks output files BED format
- Created snakemake rule uses bedtools generate new BED file containing “reproducible” peaks
- Create snakemake rule uses bedtools filter “reproducible” peaks falling blacklisted regions
- Create snakemake rule performs motif finding list filtered, reproducible peaks
- Downloaded BED file genomic coordinates refseq genes hg38 human reference genome using UCSC table Browser
- Created snakemake rule uses deeptools computeMatrix utility, aforementioned BED file hg38 genes previously generated bigWig files generate matrix signal values
- Created snakemake rule uses deeptools plotProfile utility generate simple visualization signal across hg38 genes using matrix outputs previous stepCHALLENGE
authors original paper use MEME suite tools perform motif finding. However, specify parameters used analysis input MEME list peaks (current output). optional challenge, instead performing motif finding HOMER, use MEME-ChIP paper discover enriched motifs.general, figure following:Install Meme suite conda environmentExtract chromosome sizes human gencode referenceDetermine average size peaksUse chromosome sizes, average size peaks, BedTools make BED file containing peaks expanded around ~500bp size.Extract DNA sequences regions BED file bgzipped genomic FASTA file gencode reference.Run MEME-ChIP using FASTA file DNA sequences covered peaks
code:
1. Generate a snakemake rule that runs the makeTagDir command in HOMER for each sample. You will have a separate tag directory for each sample. You may leave every option at default parameters.
    - As denoted by the name, this utility creates a directory containing a multitude of output files. Please refer to the snakemake documentation on how to properly handle using directories as expected outputs. 1. Generate a snakemake rule that runs findPeaks on your samples
    - You will need this rule to run twice on each set of paired replicates
    - Remember that Runx1 is a transcription factor that binds in relatively “narrow” patterns. 1. Generate a snakemake rule that use the pos2bed utility to convert the output to a BED format1. Using only bedtools, generate a single list of “reproducible peaks”. You may use whatever strategy you like, but justify your choice in your report. 1. Create a snakemake rule that uses bedtools to filter out any of your reproducible peaks that fall into these blacklisted regions and output the remaining peaks to a new BED file1. Use the annotatePeaks.pl utility in HOMER to annotate your list of reproducible peaks.
    - This utility will create a simple TSV / .txt file of annotated peaks1. Run findMotifsGenome.pl on your list of reproducible peaks to generate a motif enrichment analysis1. Using the bigwig files for the IP samples generated earlier and the BED file of hg38 genes you just downloaded in BED format, run the computeMatrix utility in DeepTools in the scale-regions mode twice (once for each IP sample).
    - Remember that we are trying to reproduce the figure from the paper, be sure to include regions in a 2kb window up- and downstream of the TSS and TTS, respectively. You may leave every other parameter at its default value. Justify any changes in parameters if you do alter them.

2. You will have run computeMatrix twice (on both IP replicates) and generated two matrices of values. Run plotProfile on each matrix to generate a visualization of the Runx1 signal coverage averaged across the body of all hg38 genes.

chapter: Project 2: ChIPseq analysis of the human transcription factor Runx1
heading: 0.11 Week 4: Integration with RNAseq and visualization
text:
Now processed performed basic analysis ChIPseq data, move attempting reproduce figure 2f, combines data generated ChIPseq experiment RNAseq experiment Figure 1. authors produced simple stacked bar chart displaying often Runx1 binding peak observed genes found differentially expressed cell line upon Runx1 knockdown using shRNA.Navigate GEO accession GSE75070 locate differential expression results RNAseq experimented figure 1.Next, generating visualizations peaks found promoter regions two key genes reported paper. Download IGV (https://software.broadinstitute.org/software/igv/) locally computer use newly added web-interface (https://igv.org/app/) load bigwig tracks (runx1_rep1, runx1_rep2, inp_rep1, inp_rep2), BED file “reproducible peaks”, human reference GTF annotation file genome browser choice.Although discouraged generating figures using screenshots, going produce figure found 2d 2e.jupyter notebook created stacked barcharts, please also generate following figures:end week 4, accomplished following:
- Download processed differential expression results RNAseq experiment performed Figure 1. Apply filters cutoffs generate dataframe containing significantly DE genes.
- Using annotated peak file list DE genes, generate stacked barchart similar figure 2f displays many DE genes Runx1 ChIPseq peak located within +/-5kb, +/-20kb, +/- 100kb TSS
- screenshot MALAT1 promoter region displaying 4 bigWig files signal coverage (runx1_rep1, runx1_rep2, inp_rep1, inp_rep2) BED file reproducible peaks
- screenshot NEAT1 promoter region displaying 4 bigWig files signal coverage (runx1_rep1, runx1_rep2, inp_rep1, inp_rep2) BED file reproducible peaks
- formatted table containing number raw mapped reads samples akin Supplementary Figure 2A
- venn diagram indicating many peaks called replicate experiment intersection representing many peaks considered “reproducible” using chosen strategy / thresholdChallengeThis paper chosen intentionally sections published methods sparse. One example methods used generate supplementary figure S6A, bar graph representing “% overlapping runx1 peaks” various transcription factors histone marks. ’ll notice publication describe figure generated.best recreate figure, limit top 5 factors (AP2g, DNase1, AE2F1, MAX, PML)
code:
1. Download the DESeq2 results (GSE75070_MCF7_shRUNX1_shNS_RNAseq_log2_foldchange.txt.gz) and upload this file to the SCC.
2. In a jupyter notebook, apply the same filters and cutoffs as specified in the methods of the original paper. How many DE genes do you find? Do they match the numbers reported in the paper?
3. Using the list of DE genes downloaded in step 1 and the annotated peak file you generated previously, recreate figure 2f and supplementary figure S2D and produce a single stacked barchart showing the proportions of DE genes with a Runx1 peak found within +/- 5kb, +/- 20kb, and +/- 100kb of the TSS
    - N.B. In their figure 2f, they calculate one set of numbers relative to the TSS and another set relative to the ‘whole body’ of the gene. For our case, just calculate all of these values using the distance to TSS found in your annotations file.1. Navigate to the two genes mentioned specifically in the paper, MALAT1 and NEAT1. 
    - Make sure to answer the following questions in your written report: 
    - Do you see the same general results as in figures 2d and 2e?
    - What does this figure imply? 
    - Do you agree with the conclusions made by the authors?1. Please take a screenshot of the MALAT1 and NEAT1 promoter regions displaying the signal coverage found in your bigWig files and the peak locations from your BED file of reproducible peaks.
    - We will work in class on improving these figures to be publication-worthy1. Similar to Supplementary Figure S2A, generate a nicely formatted table that displays the number of raw reads and number of mapped reads for each sample
2. Similar to Supplementary Figure S2C, generate a venn diagram indicating how many peaks you discovered in each replicate experiment with the intersection representing the number of peaks you chose as your set of “reproducible” peaks

chapter: Project 2: ChIPseq analysis of the human transcription factor Runx1
heading: 0.12 Week 5: Writing a report
text: Full writeup instructions can found : Project Writeup InstructionsNow completed week tasks, working pipeline processes full ChIPseq experiment raw data peak calling. Now need write report details entire project style scientific publication. reminder, general outline structure :
code:
Introduction (1-2 paragraphs)
    - What is the biological background of the study?
    - Why was the study performed?
    - What main experimental techniques were used in the study?

Methods (variable)
    - Describe any specific parameters used, software versions, or specific steps that would ultimately affect the reproducibility of the results

Results (variable)
    - Results from each step in the methods with appropriate figures / tables containing descriptive captions

Discussion (1 page)
    - Discuss and interpret the results in the context of the biological question asked by the study. Briefly state the main results and follow-on with a discussion on the implications and the biological conclusions drawn that are supported by the results. 
    - Briefly discuss how well you managed to reproduce the results from the paper

Future Directions (1-2 paragraphs)
- List at least three follow-up experiments / questions that would be interesting to follow-up on after the paper
- What would these experiments attempt to answer? And why would they be relevant?

Challenges (1 paragraph)
- What were the main difficulties you encountered in the project?

Conclusion (1 paragraph)
- What is the main conclusion a reader should draw from the analysis?

References
- Use consistent, proper citation format for any references (Mendeley, Zotero, Paperpile)

chapter: Project 3: Single Cell Analysis of the Mouse Kidney
heading: 0.13 Week 1: Getting Started
text: Read paperThis analysis based data published study: Park, J., Shrestha, R., Qiu, C., Kondo, ., Huang, S., Werth, M., Li, M., Barasch, J., & Suszták, K. (2018). Single-cell transcriptomics mouse kidney reveals potential cellular targets kidney disease. Science, 360(6390), 758–763.Please read paper get sense overall hypotheses, goals, conclusions presented. focusing mostly results presented first figure.Downloading dataThis publication encompasses 7 total samples analyzed single cell RNA sequencing working four true wild-type samples. convenience concern disk space, downloaded processed 3 4 samples meeting criteria.Individually, please download data associated SRX3436301.may find following resource helpful preparing data processing (https://kb.10xgenomics.com/hc/en-us/articles/115003802691----prepare-Sequence-Read-Archive-SRA-data--NCBI--Cell-Ranger-).recall, early processing steps single cell RNA sequencing generally bulk RNA sequencing. aligning reads appropriate reference genome quantifying generating counts matrix. case single cell RNAseq, counts matrix matrix cells x genes opposed genes x samples. data generated 10X platform, using custom software suite CellRanger pre-process prior analysis.Now successfully aligned sample reference genome. going combine samples generate single feature matrix can used downstream analysis.two main utilities post-alignment analysis single cell data: 1. Scanpy 2. Seurat. feature-rich, intuitive, offer number pre-built functions perform many standardized operations analysis single cell RNA sequencing data. following steps, recommended create Jupyter Notebook (Lab) Rmarkdown notebook using either Scanpy Seurat, respectively, perform analyses.Single Cell RNA AnalysisNext walk basic downstream steps single cell RNAseq analysis required perform.
code:
1. After you have formatted your data correctly, please use CellRanger Count to generate a BAM file of alignments of the FASTQs from sample SRX3436301 to the provided mouse reference genome. 
    - You may do this using snakemake or a simple qsub script. Please ensure you set appropriate memory and time requests on SCC. 1. Using the paths provided for the processed data (materials/project_3_scrnaseq/) from the other samples and the BAM file of alignments for sample SRX3436301, use a method to “combine” these samples as a single feature matrix
    - You may consider using a utility like CellRanger Aggr or look into tool-specific utilities provided by Seurat or Scanpy1. Filter out low quality cells - There are a number of established metrics for determining “low” quality cells. 
    - Provide at least one plot that demonstrates your justifications for your chosen filtering thresholds
    - Provide a table that lists how many cells are filtered out / remain after each of your filtering thresholds

chapter: Project 3: Single Cell Analysis of the Mouse Kidney
heading: 0.14 Week 2: Downstream Analysis
code:
1. Normalize and transform your counts matrix
2. Identify highly variable genes (feature selection)
    - Report the number of features you used after filtering and justify any filtering criteria used
3. Identify clusters of cell type subpopulations. Use a clustering method of your choosing to discover clusters of cells. 
4. Identify the number of cells contributed by each sample in each cluster.
    - Display this information in an appropriate plot / table
5. Identify marker genes for each cluster. Use a differential expression method to identify the marker genes in each cluster. You may use any reasonable approach to do this.
6. Label clusters as a cell type based on marker genes. The marker genes should indicate which clusters correspond to which cell types. You may use the marker genes indicated by the authors or do your own research.
    - You may use any reasonable method to accomplish this
7. Visualize the clustered cells using a projection method. Use either t-SNE or UMAP to create a projection and scatter plot colored by cell cluster. 
    - Label the plot with the cell types you previously assigned. 
8. Visualize the top marker genes per cluster. Choose however many top marker genes you deem suitable and visualize them to show the differences.
    - Create at leaast one plot that displays the top marker genes per cluster.

Methods (variable)

    - Results from each step in the methods with appropriate figures / tables containing descriptive captions

Discussion (1 page)
    - Discuss and interpret the results in the context of the biological question asked by the study. Briefly state the main results and follow-on with a discussion on the implications and the biological conclusions drawn that are supported by the results. 
    - Briefly discuss how well you managed to reproduce the results from the paper

Future Directions (1-2 paragraphs)
- List at least three follow-up experiments / questions that would be interesting to follow-up on after the paper
- What would these experiments attempt to answer? And why would they be relevant?

Challenges (1 paragraph)
- What were the main difficulties you encountered in the project?

Conclusion (1 paragraph)
- What is the main conclusion a reader should draw from the analysis?

References
- Use consistent, proper citation format for any references (Mendeley, Zotero, Paperpile)
